phenomenon: linguistic framing effects on civic engagement motivation
claim: framing an action as identity-expression rather than mere behavior increases motivation to enact that action.

phenomenon: religious concept activation and prosocial resource allocation
claim: implicit activation of religious concepts increases anonymous giving through perceived supernatural accountability.

phenomenon: religious concept priming and risk tolerance modulation
claim: reminders of God increase risk-taking in nonmoral domains when perceived as a reliable safety-provider, reversing the typical religiosity-risk relationship.

phenomenon: retrieval practice effects on memory as a function of task conditions
claim: testing produces memory enhancement or impairment depending on retrieval demand and learner factors, unified by a multifactor account.

phenomenon: visual dominance in expert evaluative judgment
claim: visual information automatically dominates judgment and prediction of performance quality even when experts consciously prioritize alternative modalities.

phenomenon: affective state influence on well-being self-evaluation
claim: current affective state colors subjective well-being evaluations through mood-congruent judgment.

phenomenon: arousal misattribution and sexual attraction formation
claim: heightened arousal from threat or fear can be misattributed to attraction toward nearby potential partners.

phenomenon: neural asymmetry and decision-making bias modulation
claim: right-hemisphere dominance (inconsistent handedness) promotes flexible belief revision and reduced status quo bias relative to consistent right-hand dominance.

phenomenon: sequential request compliance and self-commitment escalation
claim: agreeing to a small request increases compliance with subsequent larger requests via internalized self-concept alignment.

phenomenon: gustatory-sensory priming of moral judgment severity
claim: bitter taste sensations trigger harsher moral judgments through embodied disgust, particularly among politically conservative individuals.

phenomenon: unconscious scene-object semantic integration
claim: visual integration of objects with their contextual scenes and semantic appropriateness occurs outside conscious awareness.

phenomenon: chromatic priming and cognitive-task performance differential
claim: red enhances detail-oriented performance through avoidance motivation while blue enhances creative performance through approach motivation.

phenomenon: organic-food exposure and moral identity affirmation effects
claim: exposure to organic foods triggers moral self-affirmation that paradoxically reduces subsequent helping behavior.

phenomenon: ovulatory-cycle modulation of political preferences by relationship status
claim: women's fertility cycle shifts political and religious views differentially by relationship status, with ovulation promoting liberalism in single women and conservatism in partnered women.

phenomenon: control-threat coping and system-justification escalation
claim: threats to perceived personal control increase belief in powerful external systems (divine and socio-political) as compensatory control mechanisms.

phenomenon: vertical spatial representation of social hierarchy
claim: social rank is automatically represented as vertical position, facilitating faster processing and inference of hierarchical relations.

phenomenon: tax-aversion as distinct from rational cost-avoidance
claim: people irrationally prefer to avoid tax-labeled costs more than equivalent or larger non-tax costs, driven by political identity.

phenomenon: phonemic-shape correspondence prior to visual awareness
claim: sound-shape mapping (bouba-kiki effect) operates preconsciously and facilitates breakthrough to awareness of matching visual forms.

phenomenon: domain-generality of syntactic structural processing in neural response
claim: language and music evoke neurally indistinguishable responses to structural incongruity, challenging domain-specific theories of syntax.

phenomenon: lower socioeconomic status and elevated prosocial behavior orientation
claim: individuals from lower socioeconomic backgrounds exhibit greater generosity, charity, and trust than upper-class counterparts through egalitarian value commitment.
